sentences.text: Yes.
Well, in 19-- I believe it started either in late 1939 or 1940.
I unfortunately do not remember exactly.
But every Jew had to wear a yellow star that is-- said Jude, Jew, in German letters, which were written like Hebrew characters, with the thickening on the crosspieces and-- and so on.
And everybody had to wear it.
And I [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] did not want to.
And being very stubborn and headstrong and very stupid and young at the time, decided I wasn't going to wear it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it-- it-- it was a-- a crime punishable by death.
If-- if-- if-- if-- if some-- if they found somebody, the whole family was sent to-- to-- was sent away.
We didn't know about concentration camps.
I had no idea these places existed.
They were sent to Poland, and nobody was ever heard from again.
So we knew, but we thought they were just locking them up, or they were in jail.
We didn't know they were gassing them.
We were not-- this-- this was not aware.
We were only-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] listening to foreign radio was punishable by death.
And everyday people, if they did, they didn't tell twelve-year-old children about it, because the mouth, you know.
So I don't know.
I never knew anything that went on, except what I-- what was available through the German propaganda machine.
So anyway, to get back to the star.
I-- I decided I wasn't going to wear it.
And I really endangered my family, and I endangered everybody's life.
And I guess every once in a while something has to go right.
Nobody ever caught me, because I never wore it.
I used to have a-- a bag, like a sort of a-- a zipper case, which I used to wear.
And I-- I used to make myself a star with little hooks on it, the pins that I bent in the-- in the six corners.
And I used to pin it on me when I went out, and I used to wear the bag.

And-- and when I went out-- and as soon as I came out the door, so my father wouldn't see that I took it off, because he wouldn't have let me-- nobody would have let me walk out of the house-- I would take it off underneath the bag and put it into my pocket.
I don't know how many times I scratched my hands on the pin corner.
And on the way back, I would take it and put it on and then wear with this thing on with my handbag under my arm and go home and-- and coming there.
In all the years ever, I didn't wear it until I came to Theresienstadt.
I wasn't-- they considered it a mark of shame.
And I was going-- I was not going to wear a mark of shame, and nobody was going to make me.
I wasn't going to walk around in the street.
They were-- the people would spit at you and things like that.
And I wasn't going to let this happen, and I didn't.
It was not one-- it's not one of the proudest moments in my life, but I was lucky.
I didn't do it.
[LAUGHS] Well, when I was-- in the year 1940, when I was 14 years old, I-- they wanted to have agricultural workers for-- well, they-- they-- they took Jews.
They took a bunch of us girls, and they sent us to a agricultural camp.
Actually, it was a big farm, and they-- you took one of the barns, and they made a-- a girls' camp out of it.
They put some beds in there, some iron bedsteads and straw sacks, and that's-- that was our home.
We were about 30-- some 30-some-odd girls, 32, something like that, all Jewish girls between 14 and 16, or between 13 and 16, really.
I wasn't one of the youngest.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were sent to this place in Silesia.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH].
It-- it is not important.
It was in a-- it was-- it was in a county which was called Gardelegen.
And Gardelegen became a very well-known concentration camp later on.
But I don't think it had any connection to that farm where we were sitting.
It was a place called [NON-ENGLISH], [?
Kreis ?] Gardelegen. 'Kreis' is the equivalent of 'county' in English.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was a small agricultural thing.
I wasn't really in a village.
We were on a farm out in the middle of nowhere in this barn.
And we were-- we were harvesting asparagus.
Well, asparagus grows very funny.
It grows in-- in furrows like little, I don't know, little mountains or sort of rows, which are approximately less than a foot high.
So they're little hills that go along for [? ends. ?] And you get a knife, which looks like a chisel with a bent sort of a thing and has a-- has a knife at the bottom.
And asparagus, when it comes-- at that time in Austria, in-- in-- in Germany, asparagus had to be white.
And when it breaks the ground, it becomes green, and then it gets tough.
So you have to harvest it while it is still underground.
So what it is, the point comes to the top of the earth-- it's sort of sandy soil-- and it cracks it.
And when you see a crack, you take two fingers, and you go and you dig down on the both sides of the stalk, and then you take your knife into it.
You have to go all the way down so you-- and you have to stand up and do this.
You can't kneel down because you'll have-- you'll ruin the-- the-- you can only be in the narrow strip between the beds.
So you have to stand up being bent over like this.
That was the beginning of my back treatments.
There are more to follow.
And so we had to harvest asparagus one stalk at the time for, I don't know, 6, 8 hours a day.
And we were lucky, because all the-- our camp was lucky.
I had had scarlet fever in 1938.
My brother had it, and I caught it from him, and that's why we were stuck.
And I was in the hospital.
I was very, very sick.
I had a lot of complications.
And we were-- I was in the hospital until after July.
So we really couldn't do anything.
Even if we would have had an opportunity, we could not go.
But there were two sick children in the house from March until May and from May until the end of July.
So-- so we-- we-- we developed-- somebody-- out of the 30-some-odd girls, 12 or 15 had scarlet fever.
And they were afraid we were going to contaminate the whole neighborhood.
Everybody was going.
And scarlet fever, at that time, was a very serious children's infection.
And it was before sulfas or penicillin, so strep was bad.
So they-- this-- all the other groups, the older children, they were all sent on to Poland.
None of them came back.
Our group, they wanted to get us out of there.
They didn't want us.
And all the people-- the sanitary conditions were bad.
So we would have been, you know, bad.
They-- everybody where we were made the whole countryside sick.
So they sent us back to Vienna.
It was a nightmarish trip, took 55 hours where I was sleeping standing up in the train.
But anyway, we were in a sealed car, and they sent us back to Vienna.
When I was back in Vienna, they had my name, and they-- I had to work.
And I was assigned to an old paper factory.
May I ask first, this was forced labor?
You were taken away to Germany?
You were [? forced to-- ?]
Absolutely, it was forced labor.
I didn't want to do it.
I was 14 years old.
I didn't-- and my parents didn't want me to go and harvest asparagus and break my back and-- and live on asparagus. 90% percent of the food we got was asparagus.
Scary, very scary, but very unavoidable.
And you-- you started, sort of, to live in a-- in a-- in a stupor.
You did what you were told, because you had absolutely-- what was I going to do?
What were my parents going to do?
If they would say I can't go, somebody was going to kill them.
I mean, nobody that is not involved in this can fathom, no matter how much empathy you have, what it would be like to be subjected to conditions like that.
Because this is really unimaginable until you're stuck with it.
So I went.
We were there.
And so we cried a lot, and we talked to one another and we developed friendships and we sort of held one another up.
We were all little girls.
We were all afraid.
We were all away from mommy and daddy.
And nobody really gave a damn if we lived or died.
They fed us asparagus and jam.
When they cooked meat, they cooked it with marmalade.
And it was-- it was not--
I don't know, some women.
Some sort of [NON-ENGLISH] type women, you know.
I don't know.
This came from later.
I never saw it.
I had never lived this way.
You did what you were told.
You went out in the morning.
You did it.
You came home.
You laid down.
You cried a little bit.
You ate a little bit.
You fell asleep.
You cried a little bit.
You got up.
You went to-- you went to work.
You dug your thing, and you cried a little bit.
I-- I don't want to think about it.
Let's-- let's leave that.
It was too early.
I wasn't-- I didn't have any-- any-- any defenses yet.
It is-- it's-- it's open.
It's-- it's not-- I don't-- enough.
Anyway, we came back to Vienna.
They had my name.
So I was assigned to this paper factory, Bunzl and Biach.
It had been a Jewish firm-- B-U-N-Z-L and B-I-A-C-H.
It had been a Jewish firm.
They were taking old paper and making new paper out of it.
They were melting it down or whatever and stamping out new paper.

And in order to get the-- the old paper into the factory, there were-- there were trucks and horse-drawn wagons sent out to the different places that had old paper to sell-- old business papers, stocks, and papers and old records and old books and things like that.
They were usually kept in-- in cellars and downstairs or in back, you know, backrooms or back offices.
And I was assigned to a-- a horse-drawn wagon with two other Jewish girls.
There were three of us.
And we were to get the old paper, bring it onto the-- onto the-- the-- the wagon, you know, load it onto the wagon, where it was taken back to the factory and unloaded.
And then they were doing-- they were sorting it and putting it [INAUDIBLE] with linen, without linen, cardboard, whatever.
And I was there from August 19-- from August 1940-- because my mother was still alive.
So I remember when I came back.
But I went home every night, until, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think, '42.
Yeah, sort of like September '42, something like that.
My job consisted out of put the-- we had these round baskets and-- or bales.
We had like a stevedore's hook.
The bales were cardboard and cottons and things like that.
And loose papers, like letters and books and things, went in the baskets.
And you had to carry the basket and hold it between your neck, behind your-- you know, over your shoulders and carry it up the stairs and then push it over the side of the wagon and drop it in there.
Or the bale, you had to go, you know, put it with your hook and go up with the bale.
The-- the bales weighed 90 kilos, which is 200 pounds, and I weighed approximately 95 pounds at the time.
I was 14 years old.
And the baskets weighed about 60 kilos, which is, I don't know, 150 pounds, something like that, give or take a couple of pounds.
It doesn't pay to figure it out too exactly.
And that was my life.
And every evening I went home, and every morning I went.
And we sat there in snow.
And sometimes it would take us two hours in the wagon to get there.
And we sat there in the back of the wagon in snow and rain and summer, winter.
And that's why today I have scoliosis in two places, a misplaced vertebrae, and I'm-- my-- my-- my body's twisted like a pretzel.
I was carrying twice my own weight for two years.
The first time I picked it up, I-- they put a-- a thing on-- on-- on me, and I flew back.
I made a somersault in back over the basket, because I was-- it was much too heavy for me.
How was I able to carry?
I learned how because I knew I had to.
And if I didn't carry it, somebody was going to hit me, so I carried it.
I did one step at a time.
And somebody says, don't do this and don't breathe like that.
And-- I don't know, I did.
And I became as strong as an ox.
I was very, very strong at that.
I was young, and I was healthy.
And the physical exercise, aside from the fact that it ruined my bones, didn't-- didn't do me any harm.
And I came home every night, and I had a decent bed to sleep in and whatever food there was.
We-- I was living at home.
There were-- if you live for all the time in a place, you have ways of get-- getting more food than they allow you.
So we didn't eat like we had eaten before, but I never went hungry, not once.

Instead of eating, I don't know, veal cutlet, I might be eating a piece of fish, if it was available, or-- or-- or somebody had a chicken, or-- some-- some [INAUDIBLE] you-- you took a piece of jewelry and-- and bought yourself a goose someplace or-- if you have lived in a place all your life, you have-- there are ways of knowing what to do and where to go and-- everything.
Jews were dogs.
They were not allowed to sit on park benches.
They were not allowed to go to movies.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were not allowed to use public transportation.
They were not allowed to go to swimming pools.
They were not allowed to go to theaters.
They got smaller ration cards than other people-- lesser portions.
Other people got clothing cards, you know, for clothing, coupons.
Jews did not get that.
Jews were required to turn in all their jewelry.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was ashamed because people-- everybody was doing what-- nobody ever said-- we didn't know, in our beleaguered little thing there, that what went down in the rest of the world.
I never, until after-- way after the war, when I found out that there was a resistance here or this was there, Warsaw ghetto.
I never heard of anything.
All I saw was a bunch of people which I had been respecting and everything going like lambs to the slaughter and saying, yes, sir, and yes, sir, and yes, sir.
And it was very, very disturbing.
And I was working like a dog, and-- and I wasn't allowed to go to school.
And-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was a very hard-- and you could-- I-- I don't know.
It never occurred to me that this was going to last for as long as it did.
But then again, I was young and stupid.
I didn't know whatever what I was dealing with.
I didn't understand politics.
All I knew is that-- that I was in a living-- I was living in a nightmare, and there was no thing.
And my-- my father, he managed to keep my brother with him and taught him, you know, with working.
And he got himself-- through some connections, he got himself assigned to a-- a-- a carpentry shop, and he was working there.

And the-- the head of the Gestapo in Vienna was a-- the Jewish-- of the Jewish department, the-- the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH] but the Jewish department of the Gestapo in Vienna, was a man named Kuchman, and he-- my father had done his villa.
And when he became the big shot, when there were lists made for-- for people being sent away to concentration camp, we were not on them.
Most of the people were sent in '41 and '42.
They had transports of 1,000 people.

I would say by-- by 19-- by-- by September, October 1942 when the last transports left Vienna, 90% of the Jews, 95% of the Jews were-- were-- there were about 300,000 Jews in Austria, of which 200-- approximately-- I don't-- approximately 200,000 were in Vienna.
In 1938, all the Jews from all over Austria had to come to Vienna, and they-- they started to send them out.

And by the end of-- by-- or September, October 1942, 95% of what was left was-- you know, what had not been able to get out in '38 and '39 when immigration, if you had somebody or some special case, was possible.
One of my father's sister got out and went to Israel.
But the rest of us, we didn't have any relatives.
Nobody from our family had ever emigrated to any place.
We had a good life at home, so we didn't have anybody outside.
So we had paid landing money for a permit to Australia.
And we were supposed to leave sometimes in-- in-- in-- in-- was it like-- in October, the 8th or the 10th of October 1939.
And then the war came before this, so there was no way of getting out of Austria.
My father had had some money that was not in Austria in a bank.
It was in a bank in Switzerland.
And he was able to pay, you know.
I don't know.
It was not much.
It was about 100, 150 English pounds a head so that they would let you in.
And we had-- we were supposed to go to Melbourne in Australia.
Well, of course, the war started in-- in-- in September of-- September '39.
So that was the end of that.
So we never got out.
My-- well, there were-- we were living very close together.
In our house, there was my father, my brother, my mother, and I.
There was also another person who was sort of a family relative.
She was my cousin.
She was-- she was a first cousin-- her-- her mother was a first cousin of my father.
Our grandmother were sisters.
And she was like my oldest sister.
She lost her parents.
Her father had a big business in Yugoslavia, and she was born in Yugoslavia.
That saved-- that saved her from a lot of grief later on.
Because in 1938, she was 18 years old, and she was able to get out.
She had a Yugoslav passport.
She was able to get out to England.
Her name was Mira.
It still is.
It still is.
I saw her last-- last summer.
We were all together in Israel, the three of us, my brother, she, and I.
And being that she lives in Australia now, and my brother lives in Israel, and I live in New York, it was a special occasion.
Our grandmothers were sisters.
My grandmother, my father's mother, and her mother's mother were sisters.
And we were a very close family.
So everybody was-- anyway.
She was in Yugoslavia.
And when she was born, my father's older sisters-- her mother was sort of overwhelmed by the big household.
She called on her-- her cousin, which they were very close together, to come there.
And she, my Aunt [NON-ENGLISH], she never had any children of her own, but she was a mother if ever there was one.
She was a wonderful woman.
We all adored her.
And everybody-- the first word that Mira ever spoke was [NON-ENGLISH], which means aunt in-- in-- in Yugoslav or Slavic or whatever they were speaking at that-- that-- in that place.
And there was Mira.
So she was-- and Mira was like my older sister, because she was six-- six years older than I am.
And I was forever staying up with [NON-ENGLISH] and Mira in their house.
I was visiting there almost every weekend.
I went there on Yom Kippur, because nobody was eating anything.
And they packed a little chicken, put a little bread, when I was a kid before I had to [? pass, ?] in a napkin, and they took me up there.
And I went to the-- so Mira could watch me so they could go to temple and, you know, and things like that.
And she used to take me to the amusement park.
Because she was six years older than I was, so it was easy.
She-- we have a very good relationship.
And then she went to England, and she married there during the war.
And I didn't see her again until 1962, and I had said good-bye to her in 1938.
And when I saw her, it was like as if we had seen one another yesterday.
It was 25 years or something, but it was like it was yesterday.
We started to pick up as if there was nothing.
It was-- it was wonderful.
And then I kept going to England every year, like taking a bus every year.
I was there once or twice.
It was only $200 at the time, you know.
So I kept going back and forth and visiting.
And then her-- some of her children decided to move to Australia, because in England the economy wasn't so hot.
So she went to visit them, and she decided that she was going too.
Her husband decided they wanted to be there.
They wanted-- she has two children that she had early, and then 10 years later she had two more.
She left--
--she left Vienna in 1938 to become a maid in somebody's household in England.
I had friends that-- I had-- everybody that I knew was taken in the first waves of deportation.
When we were left, there were only maybe no more than 300 Jew-- Jewish people.
I'm not talking families.
I'm talking about heads left in Vienna.
And actually, there were approximately 90 people-- 90 Jews left in Vienna that were never sent away.
I don't know how they-- anyway, Mr.
Kuchman made sure that we were not in any of the transports.
And when Mr.
Kuchman was transferred in the fall of 1943, all of a sudden we came on the transport, the next transport.
We-- we were-- at that time, we didn't go on a transport.
We were just in a about 100 families-- or people, I don't remember anymore.
We were taken in a regular railroad car and sent to Theresienstadt.
It-- this was life.
There was-- there was-- you-- you adjust yourself.
There were no synagogues.
They were all burned in 1938, so there hadn't been.
They had-- in my-- my building there was the-- they had-- they called it the little temple.
There was-- there had been a small chapel or whatever you call it.
I don't know.
In-- you know, they had a different name for it-- in the-- in the ground floor of where we lived.
So this was something that belonged to the Jewish community center, so nobody came in there to interfere with it.
So they-- they-- they didn't have any-- they had a Torah, and they had a-- a-- they had a-- they-- they-- they met.
A few people met there every-- every Shabbat for things.
And my brother was bar mitzvahed there and-- and things like that.
But it was-- there-- there was nobody left.
And half the people that were left probably didn't want to pray.
So you lived your little life in this apartment that we got.
I'm sorry.
In this apartment that we got, there were-- there were-- we had-- we had two rooms.
My aunt-- the one Mira-- the one that-- the two people that wound up being Mira's parents had another very big room.
And my-- my uncle that had to come back from-- the oldest brother had left in [NON-ENGLISH]-- he had to come back to Vienna.
Then there was another woman.
Her husband had managed to get out of the country, and she was there with her little boy, Mrs.
[NON-ENGLISH], and she had a five-year-old boy and her mother.
They were also from [NON-ENGLISH].
They lived in another room.
And a family named Schwartz lived in a kitchen and another bedroom.
And it-- it was a gypsy existence.
And-- and all of us had a bathroom on the floor that had been a bathroom for the-- for the-- for the-- for the [NON-ENGLISH].
And that's-- that's how we lived, and that's what was there-- there was and since there was nothing else.
And my-- I remember my father was-- was fantastic.
He managed to build us a little refrigerator.
It was the-- about the size of this little table here, a little square box.
And we-- we could-- we could have milk in the house that we could buy, if we got some that didn't go bad.
Because there were no refrigerators.
There were no cooking stove.
Everything was makeshift.
We had-- the closets we had where we hung out clothes were out of some [NON-ENGLISH], the book closets.
We had to leave all our furniture behind.
Everything was makeshift.
No bed-- no-- nothing fit with one another and no silverware, no china, nothing-- five plates.
It-- it was a mess.
But this-- this isn't what count.
What counts is whether you survive and that you live from day to day.
And nobody beats you, and nobody injures you.
They work you to death, but we were used to that.
We were living in hell, and those-- those were the rules that-- that-- I-- I don't know.
I did stupid things because I'm stupid.
We-- we-- well, I was young and-- and ignorant.
I remember what we did.
I figured it out.

What we did is we printed some things, some anti-something, and-- and we-- and-- and I had the idea of going to an el station and putting it on a-- on a-- on one of the posts, you know, there on those things.
They had like these marble things, and I put it on the top there.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- and-- and-- and-- and-- and-- and disappeared.
And when the train came, the wind pushed the leaves away.
We were long gone.
If anybody would have seen-- and then they were flying all over, of course.
They were flying, and they came down to the street, you know, like an elevator train like that.
And of course, they found out where they were coming from.
It didn't last long.
But-- but the things like that, I could have killed everybody.
What did who say?
They-- they were-- they were some anti [INAUDIBLE].
They said something about the battle being lost, that somebody had heard over the-- the shortwave radio and told us.
And we made a little one-page thing.
We wrote them on pieces of paper.
Print them.
Who had a printing machine?
We were kids.
But some of those things happened.
